lines.text: Good evening, Madison. PRGE. The clock reads 19:00, and yes, we are live.
For those of you tuning in for the first time — welcome to the one night a year we get to say exactly what we mean without the FCC or the Vance Administration breathing down our necks.
I'm Quinn Foley. Beside me is the greatest weather forecaster in Madison, Caroline. Across the room, Holden, who is currently running the stats. And Tim, without whom none of this happens.
The Purge commenced at 19:00. We are {{ELAPSED}} in. Twelve hours remain.
I'm going to level with you — I am four whiskeys in and it is not even 19:30. This is either a very good sign or a very bad one.
Let's get you the weather before anything else matters. Caroline, you've been studying tonight's conditions all week. What are we walking into?
Clear skies right now, 41 degrees, southwest wind at 8 miles per hour.
But that's not the story. The story is what's coming at us between 23:00 and 01:00.
I'm predicting a cold front arrival right around 01:00 — could be as early as 00:30, could slip to 01:30, but call it 01:00 for planning purposes.
When it arrives, we're looking at a 15-degree drop. Wind gusts to 22 miles per hour. Rain transitioning to sleet by 01:30.
That's going to suppress activity. People barricade tighter when it's cold. It makes a difference.
So we're looking at a hot first few hours, then weather brakes on around midnight. That matches what we saw last year?
Last year's front arrived at 01:12. I called it for 01:00 to 01:30. I was correct.
She was. I was there. She said it. I remember because she made me write it down.
Quinn, if you're going to drink that much, can you at least hydrate between segments?
Holden, I will take that under advisement. Go ahead — what are we looking at statistically for tonight?
Last year, 19:00 to 20:00, we recorded 8 kills across Madison. Slow start. Settling-in hour. The real action begins after 20:00.
Hour two last year spiked to 11 additional kills. That's when the East Side lit up.
My model suggests we'll see similar pacing if people follow historical behavioral patterns. But weather variations and weapon-availability shifts could change that.
Translation: we have no idea what's coming. Holden, just admit it.
We have every idea. We just can't predict it.
That's the most honest thing anyone's said all night. Tim, are we good? Technical check?
Signal's clean. Antenna's locked. All six studio mics hot and active.
Jammer sweepers detected three FCC probes in the past 48 hours — normal pre-Purge noise. Federal isn't expecting us. They never do.
Good. So we can say what we need to say tonight without interruption. Which, frankly, feels like a miracle.
Here's what's happening right now: The Purge is active. Everywhere in Madison, every neighborhood, every house — the law is suspended.
For the 71,000 of you listening last year, welcome back. For the new folks — stick with us. We're the only voice on the air right now that gives a damn whether you make it to 07:00.
That's genuinely grim, Quinn.
It's the truth. I apologize for nothing.
On a lighter note, last year the Packers moved to Tennessee. Tonight we're honoring that loss with a moment of silence.
We're not doing that.
Worth asking.
Quinn, your glass is going to tip if you lean that far back in your chair.
Tim, I have faith in the chair. The chair and I have a relationship.
Alright, listeners. First reports are coming in. Let's get you the lay of the land right now, {{ELAPSED}} into the night.
Visibility is excellent right now, but that won't hold. The smoke situation is developing.
Multiple fires reported — I count at least four structure fires on the South Side, one vehicle fire near the Beltline interchange.
South Side's been historically quieter than East Side. That might indicate organized activity rather than random.
Or it might mean nothing. Holden finds patterns in everything. Last year he spent twenty minutes explaining why the kill count peaked at 22 instead of 21.
It was statistically significant.
It was two people, Holden.
The smoke smell is carrying across the isthmus. I'm getting reports from West Side that visibility is dropping. Could be a problem if that pushes east by midnight.
It won't. Wind is from the southwest. The smoke will move north-northeast, across the water.
What you're smelling on the West Side is lake-side bleed-off. It'll clear once the front comes through.
So we're going to have poor visibility, then it gets worse, then the cold clears it?
Essentially, yes.
I love weather. It's the one thing that makes sense.
Speaking of things that don't make sense, the Capitol Square is reporting a perimeter breach attempt 23 minutes ago.
They always try the Capitol first. It's like watching people bang on a vault door at 19:00 on Purge Night.
Security held. No breach. But the fact that it happened this early suggests coordination.
We're getting a live field update in about three minutes. Tucker's positioning near the Capitol Square to give us a real-time status.
Tucker is a badass. I say that with genuine admiration and genuine concern for their safety.
They're the calmest person on this station. If anyone can move through a hot zone, it's them.
Statistically, field reporters have a 7% higher mortality rate on Purge Night than the general population.
Holden, not helping.
I'm not trying to help. I'm being accurate.
Here's what we're going to do: We're going to take a quick look at what listeners are texting in, then we'll move to the field update when Tucker is ready.
Text-in from someone in Atwood: 'Are the PurgeShield panels actually rated for 19:00 plus twelve hours or is that marketing?' Good question.
They're rated for continuous breach resistance up to 15 hours. They're built assuming some people stay locked down longer than the official window.
So if you've got PurgeShield, you're probably fine. If you've got duct tape and prayer, well, you're probably listening to us.
Another text: 'Wind shift concerns?' My forecast stands. You're good.
Text from a listener on the East Side: 'Is 20% increase in activity normal for the East Side in an early-hour Purge?' Define normal.
I love how Holden answers questions by making them bigger.
The East Side has been trending hotter for four consecutive years. This is consistent with that trend.
Whether that's normalized territorial violence, gang activity, or demographic shifts — I don't have data to distinguish.
But it's something people on the East Side are already doing differently than they were four years ago.
Yes.
Tucker is in position. They're ready for the field report.
Perfect. We're {{ELAPSED}} in, it's 19:00 and change, and Tucker Traywick is about to tell us what the ground looks like right now.
Tucker, you there?
Take your time. We can wait. We've got twelve hours.
Is Tucker's mic working?
Signal is live. They might be moving.
Field reporters often take 30 to 45 seconds to find a safe position before broadcasting. Standard protocol.
While we wait, let me remind folks that this is not a news service in the traditional sense. This is not the mainstream media — this is us, on the ground, trying to keep you alive.
The information we give you is real. The guests you'll hear from are real. The commercials are for actual services that saved actual people's lives last year.
And if you're out there and you want to call in — call in. We're monitoring lines all night.
We've got three live lines open. Standard number. No need to jam the system.
Though calling in does require nerve. Last year Darren from Atwood held the line for eleven minutes just talking to Quinn about his neighbor's broken PurgeShield panels.
Darren was terrified. He needed a voice. I was glad to be it.
Tucker is ready.
Tucker, live from Capitol Square. What do you see?
The fact that Tucker can broadcast from there suggests the immediate perimeter is clear.
Or they're taking a calculated risk.
That too.
Here's the thing about Purge Night that nobody says out loud: it's boring for the first two hours.
People are still eating dinner. Still processing the shock. It takes time for the night to *become* what it becomes.
By 21:00, that changes. By midnight, the entire city is a different animal.
Which is why weather matters. The cold front at 01:00 will hit people when they're already exhausted.
That's when behavior shifts. That's when people start heading home.
We're getting preliminary casualty reports from the East Side. I'll run numbers as they come in.
Tim, are you getting reports or assumptions?
Reports. Confirmed by neighborhood watch communications we monitor.
So the Atwood area is seeing activity in the first hour. That's a 47% deviation from last year.
Or it's just Atwood being Atwood. People like the East Side for a reason. Community is tighter. Blood runs thicker.
That's poetic, Quinn, but Holden's probably right. Something shifted.
Something *always* shifts. That's the Purge. It's not the same night twice.
Statistically inaccurate. There are measurable patterns across—
Holden. Let me have this one.
Fine. But you're wrong.
I know.
Tucker is breaking in from the field. Ready?
Always ready.

tickerItems.text: {{ELAPSED}} in: Madison-wide count stands at 4 confirmed. East Side reporting elevated activity.
Multiple structure fires reported South Side. Smoke carrying across isthmus toward West Side neighborhoods.
Capitol Square perimeter breach attempt at 19:23. Security held. No casualties reported at fortification.
Atwood Avenue (East Side): activity 47% above 2168 baseline hour 1. Pattern unclear — monitoring.
PurgeShield panels rated 15 hours continuous. If you installed last year, they're good. Call PurgeShield emergency line for concerns: 608-PANEL-5.
Listener from Atwood asking about panel rating specifics. Answer: PurgeShield holds. Stay barricaded.
